blocks.text: Chicken Predators
by Dan Fakkeldy
Identify, prevent, trap, and release — a Cape Breton field guide
Chapter 1 - The Morning You Find Feathers

You walk out with the coffee and the feed bucket, and something is wrong. The gate is fine. The door is shut. But there are feathers on the grass in a long, loose line, and the count at the pop hole is short by one. Or the door is open and there are feathers everywhere, and the count is short by four. Or everything looks right until you see the quiet heap of brown and red in the back corner of the run, and the hen who was top of the pecking order yesterday is gone.

The first ten minutes matter more than the next ten days. Most flock owners get the diagnosis wrong in those first ten minutes, and then they spend money and anger on the wrong fix. A neighbour's dog did it. No, a coyote. No, a hawk. You will have a theory within seconds of seeing the scene, and the theory will feel certain, and it will very often be wrong. This book is mostly about slowing that moment down.

Before you do anything else, secure the survivors. Shoo the living birds into the coop, shut the door, and count them. A flock that has just been hit is scattered and panicked, and a predator that is still nearby — and they often are, especially the small nocturnal ones — will take a second bird while you are staring at the feathers. Get the living safe first.

Then, and only then, look at the scene and do not clean it up yet. Your hands will want to tidy. Don't. The mess is evidence, and the evidence is the only honest witness you have. The feathers, the body if there is one, the tracks in the mud, the droppings, the way the wire is bent or the latch is undone — all of it is telling you who did this, when, and how they got in. You are about to become a reader of a very old kind of language, the language of something that was killed and eaten in the night, and the rest of this book is a phrasebook for that language.

Take photographs before the wind does. Phone pictures of the whole run from a few angles, close-ups of the wire and the latch, the feather scatter, any tracks, any droppings, the body if there is one and you can bear to. You will not remember the details accurately by this afternoon, and the photographs will let you compare against the identification chapters that come later. A photograph of a track in the dust is worth more than your best memory of it.

Do not move the body yet, if there is a body. The position of the body tells you as much as the wounds. A bird dragged into a corner and partly eaten in place is a different story from a bird killed in the open and carried off whole, and both are different again from three birds killed where they roosted and none of them eaten at all. That last one is the one that breaks people's hearts, because it looks senseless, and we will come back to it, because it is not senseless. It has a name and a cause and a predator behind it, and the cause is not cruelty in the way we usually mean it.

Notice the time. Predators keep different hours, and the hour of death narrows the suspect list more than almost anything else. Something that hit the flock at noon is a very short list. Something that hit at dusk is a different short list. Something that hit in the small hours of a moonless night is yet another. By the end of the third chapter of this book you will be able to read those hours the way a doctor reads a pulse.

Notice the weather, too, and the season. Snow is a gift, because snow takes the footprints of everything that walked in overnight and keeps them for you. Mud is almost as good. Dry dust holds tracks surprisingly well if the light is right. And the season matters because a predator's behaviour changes through the year — there is a hungry window in early spring when adults are feeding young, a dispersal window in autumn when juveniles are pushed out to find their own territory, and a scarcity window in deep winter when an animal that would normally never bother a coop gets desperate enough to try. Cape Breton has all of these windows, and they shape who is likely at your door in any given month.

Here is the thing to hold onto in this first hour. You are almost certainly feeling two things at once, and they pull in opposite directions. One is anger — at the predator, at yourself, at the coop that failed. The other is a kind of guilt, the feeling that you should have known, that you left the door open that one night, that you cheaped out on the wire. Both feelings are normal, and neither of them is a good guide to what to do next. Anger makes people reach for a trap before they know what they are trapping. Guilt makes people buy a new coop when the old one needed a twelve-dollar latch. The evidence is the guide. Read the scene, then act.

There is a third feeling that comes later, and it is worth naming now so it does not surprise you. It is the strange, quiet recognition that the thing that killed your hen was not being malicious. It was being a coyote, or a mink, or an eagle, exactly as it was made to be, in the habitat it was born into, which happens to be the same habitat you chose to keep chickens in. You live in western Cape Breton. You live in good, active wildlife country — coyote country, eagle country, bear country, mink country. The predators were here first, in the most literal sense, and they will be here long after this particular flock. The realistic work is not to rid the landscape of them, and not even to win, exactly. It is to make your birds a harder target than the other food that is out there, so that a hungry animal moves on. That is a solvable problem. Most of this book is about solving it well.

So stand at the coop, get the survivors locked in, take the photographs, and notice the hour and the weather. Do not clean up. Do not theorise yet, or if you must theorise, hold your theory loosely. The next chapters will give you the cast of characters you are actually dealing with here on the island, and then the method for reading the scene in front of you. By the time you finish the identification chapters, the photographs on your phone will make sense, and you will know — not guess — what you are up against.

One last thing for this first morning. Call the birds by name, if you name them, as you lock them in. It sounds sentimental, and it is, a little, but it does something useful too. It slows you down another notch. The temptation in the first hour is to move fast, to fix, to do something. The right move is almost always to move slow, to see, and to do the next correct small thing — secure, photograph, observe — before you do anything large. The people who lose a second flock in the week after the first loss are the ones who acted fast and acted wrong. The people who lose only the one are the ones who read the scene first.
Feathers on the grass are how this starts for almost everyone. It is how it started for the people who taught me what I am about to teach you. Take the photographs. We will read them together.
Chapter 2 - Who Is Actually Out There

If you read most chicken books or scroll most poultry forums, you would think every flock in North America is besieged by the same list of villains, in the same proportions, everywhere. Raccoon at the top, opossum underneath, a hawk overhead, a fox at the tree line, repeat. That list is real, and for a flock owner in Ohio or Pennsylvania it is close enough to the truth. It is not the truth here. The list of animals that can actually reach your birds on western Cape Breton is shorter than the generic list, and it is weighted differently, and pretending otherwise will make you spend money defending against things that are not coming and leave gaps for the things that are.

So this chapter is a census. Not of every animal on the island, but of the ones that have the means and the motive to take a chicken. We will go through them one at a time, quickly enough to keep the shape of the cast, and we will come back to each in the identification chapters with the specific sign each leaves behind. For now, meet them.

The Eastern coyote is here, and it is not the same animal as the western coyote you may have seen in films. The Eastern coyote is larger — often the size of a small German shepherd, thirty to forty-five pounds — because it carries wolf ancestry in its genome, a remnant of historical interbreeding as coyotes expanded eastward and met the remnant wolf population. On Cape Breton it is established across the island, from the highlands down through the river valleys and the settled coast. It is a capable, adaptable predator that will take chickens given the chance, usually by daylight or dusk rather than deep night, and it will carry a bird off whole if it can. You will hear them more than see them, and their tracks in the snow along a logging road or a field edge are common.

The red fox is here too, and in some ways it is a more common chicken predator than the coyote, because foxes live closer to people and are bolder around settlement. A red fox will work a fence line, dig under a run, and carry off a bird — often a single bird, taken at dusk or dawn, sometimes one of a series over several nights. Cape Breton has healthy fox numbers, and a fox that learns your coop is a slow food source will return until you change the math.

The mink is here, and on Cape Breton the mink is the predator that will hurt you in a way nothing else can. Mink are small, long, fast, semi-aquatic members of the weasel family, dark brown with a white chin patch, and they are abundant anywhere near water — which on this island means almost everywhere. A mink can get through a gap the width of your thumb. Once inside a coop, a mink will often kill many birds in a single night, far more than it can eat, biting each through the skull or the neck and leaving them where they fell. This is the scene that looks senseless, the one we set up in the first chapter, and we will get to why it happens. For now, know that the mink is the predator that turns a bad morning into a devastating one, and it is more common here than in most of the continent.

The short-tailed weasel, which most people on this island call the ermine in its white winter coat, is the mink's smaller cousin, and it is everywhere. An ermine is barely longer than your hand, rust-coloured above and cream below in summer, all white with a black tail tip in winter, and it can pass through a gap the width of a quarter. It eats mice and voles mostly, but it will absolutely take chickens — especially bantams, chicks, and juvenile birds — and like the mink it will kill more than it eats in a confined space. Do not underestimate the ermine because of its size. A motivated ermine can clean out a brooder in a night.

The striped skunk is present on Cape Breton, though in lower numbers than on the mainland. Skunks are not primarily chicken predators — they are grub and insect specialists, and they will happily clean out a compost pile or dig up a lawn — but they will take eggs from under a setting hen, and they will kill chicks and small juveniles if they can reach them. A skunk in the run is more often an egg thief than a bird killer, but the smell will tell you it was there long before you see any other sign.

The black bear is here, and a bear after chickens is a different order of problem. Bears are not common chicken predators in the way that foxes or mink are, but a bear that discovers a coop will tear the structure apart to get at the feed and the birds, and a torn-apart coop is the kind of damage that sends people to the hardware store for a week. Bears are most likely to be a problem in late summer and fall, when they are fattening for denning and calories are everything, and they will also simply destroy a feeder for the grain. We will spend a little time on bears, because the fix for a bear is not the same as the fix for a mink.

The bobcat is here, in the rougher country and the highland margins, and it will occasionally take a free-ranging bird. Bobcats are not a primary chicken predator for most backyard flocks, but they are present on the island and they are capable, and it is worth knowing their sign so you do not confuse a bobcat kill with a coyote or a dog.

The bald eagle is here, and this is where the Cape Breton list really diverges from the generic guides. Bald eagles are common on this island — year-round residents, not just migrants — and they are far more willing to take live prey than most people assume. A bald eagle will take a full-grown hen off an open field in daylight, and on Cape Breton the eagle pressure on free-ranging birds is genuinely high, higher than almost anywhere else in Canada. There are more bald eagles per kilometre of coastline here than in most of the continent, and they are accustomed to people. If you free-range, the eagle is one of your main suspects for a daytime loss.

The great horned owl is here, and it is your main nocturnal raptor threat. Great horneds are large, powerful, year-round residents, and they hunt from a perch at the edge of woods, dropping onto anything up to the size of a rooster. They will take a roosting bird from an unsecured run on a half-lit night, and they will kill cleanly with talon punctures to the back and skull. You will rarely see the owl. You will see the puncture wounds and the plucked feathers and the absence.

There are hawks here too — Cooper's hawk, sharp-shinned hawk, red-tailed hawk — and they are a real threat to chicks, bantams, and small juveniles, and an occasional threat to a small full-grown bird. Cooper's and sharp-shinned are bird-hunting specialists, built for speed through cover, and they are the ones most likely to hit a free-ranging flock at midday. For adult standard-size birds they are less of a concern than the eagle, but they will shape your chick-rearing season.

The domestic dog is here, as it is everywhere people are, and I want you to treat it as the first name on the list, not the last. Loose and roaming dogs are the single most destructive predator of backyard chickens across all of North America, and Cape Breton is no exception. A dog does not kill the way a wild predator kills. A dog kills in play and in chase, mauling birds across a wide area, often in daylight, often leaving wounded and dying birds scattered rather than carrying any off. One loose dog can end a flock in an afternoon. We will come back to the dog, because reading a dog scene is a specific skill, and because the fix is as much a matter of neighbours and bylaws as it is of fencing.

Rats are here, and they deserve a mention because a rat problem will cost you eggs and chicks even when it never touches an adult bird. Norway rats and other rat species will tunnel under coop floors, take eggs from under hens, kill chicks in the brooder, and contaminate feed. You will not usually lose an adult hen to a rat, but you will lose the next generation, and a serious rat population will attract some of the larger predators who eat rats — which is not the chain of events you want.
That is the real cast. Notice what is not on it, because the absences matter as much as the presences.

Raccoons are present on Cape Breton, but at lower densities than on the mainland. This surprises people who have kept chickens elsewhere, because on the mainland the raccoon is the iconic chicken predator, the one that opens latches and reaches through wire and pulls heads off roosting birds. Raccoons are abundant on mainland Nova Scotia, and the Strait of Canso is not the barrier it once was — the Canso Causeway and the milder winters of recent decades have let them spread onto the island, especially in the settled areas. The Cape Breton Highlands National Park lists raccoon among its resident mammals, so they are here to stay. Your raccoon pressure is still lower than a mainland flock owner's — there is not the same century-old tradition of raccoons working the island — but you cannot treat them as absent. You should still raccoon-proof your coop, for two reasons. First, the same hardware that keeps a raccoon out keeps a mink out, so the work is not wasted. Second, raccoons are the primary rabies vector of concern on the mainland and a genuine disease risk everywhere, and good exclusion is cheap insurance. But you should know that when the generic guide says raccoon is the number one threat, the Cape Breton answer is mink first, raccoon second.

Virginia opossum, the other staple of the southern Ontario and US chicken guide, is not established on Cape Breton either. Opossums have been expanding their range northward with milder winters, and there are records on mainland Nova Scotia, but they are not a Cape Breton presence to plan around. Treat them as unlikely, and do not let their absence from your planning worry you.

Cougars, mountain lions, whatever you want to call them, are effectively absent. There is the occasional unconfirmed sighting in the Maritimes, but there is no established breeding population of cougar anywhere in Nova Scotia, and you can take the cougar off your worry list entirely. Wolves are historically extirpated. You do not have wolves on Cape Breton. The Eastern coyote fills that niche now, and it does so effectively.

What you have, then, is a short, specific list. Two canids — coyote and fox, plus the dog. Two mustelids that punch above their weight — mink and ermine. A bear, occasionally. A bobcat, rarely. Three raptors — bald eagle, great horned owl, and the hawks. A skunk, mostly for eggs. And the rats, for eggs and chicks. Nine or ten names, depending on how you count, and most of your losses will trace to four or five of them.

The rest of this book is organised around that short list. In the next chapter we start the work of telling them apart at the scene, because the identification is what tells you which fix to make, and the fix for a mink is not the fix for an eagle is not the fix for a neighbour's dog. But before we get to the method, hold this cast in your head. You live in coyote country, and eagle country, and mink country, and bear country, and the rest of it. Raccoons are here too, but at lower pressure than the mainland — and you do not live in wolf or cougar country at all. The precision will save you money and birds. The generic guide will not.
Chapter 3 - Reading The Crime Scene

In the first chapter I said you would learn to read the hours the way a doctor reads a pulse. This is the chapter where that happens. Almost every predation event leaves four kinds of evidence — the time it happened, what is left of the body, where the body is, and what is on the ground around it — and reading those four together will narrow your suspect list from the whole Cape Breton cast down to one or two animals almost every time. The method is older than chicken-keeping. It is the same method a tracker uses on any kill site, and it works because animals are creatures of habit, built in particular shapes, killing in particular ways.

Start with the hour, because the hour is the fastest filter you have. Predators sort cleanly into day shift and night shift, with a few that work the edges of light at dawn and dusk. A loss that happened in full daylight — midmorning, noon, midafternoon — comes from a very short list. The bald eagle is at the top of it. A Cooper's hawk or a sharp-shinned hawk, especially against chicks or bantams. A red fox, which hunts by day as readily as by night and is one of the few mammals bold enough to hit an active run in sunlight. A neighbour's dog, which operates on no schedule at all and is in fact the most common daytime killer. And, less commonly, a coyote in the hungry seasons or a bobcat in rough country. Notice that the daylight list is almost entirely visible, active predators. Almost nothing on it sneaks in the dark.

A loss that happened at the edges — at dawn, just after first light, or at dusk as the light goes — broadens the list. Foxes work these hours heavily. Coyotes do. The domestic dog, again, works whenever it gets loose. Great horned owls begin at dusk and hunt the first and last hours of light. This dawn-and-dusk band is the busiest time for losses, because it is when the day-shift and night-shift predators overlap and when the flock is often at its most exposed — the door has just opened or is about to close, the birds are moving slowly, and the light favours the hunter.

A loss that happened in the small hours, true dark, after the flock has settled and before first light, is a different list again. Now you are in mustelid country. The mink and the ermine work the deep night. The great horned owl is still hunting. The skunk is abroad. A fox may still be around, but the signature of a deep-night loss with multiple birds killed in the coop points hard at the small nocturnal predators, and once you have seen one such scene you do not forget it.

So the first question to answer at the scene is simply, when did this happen. You may know because you checked the flock at dusk and again at dawn. You may know because a motion light came on at three in the morning. You may only be able to narrow it to overnight, which still tells you it was not the noon eagle. Pin the hour as tightly as you can, and you have thrown away half the suspect list before you look at anything else.

The second question is whether the bird is there or gone. This single fact tells you an enormous amount. A bird that has vanished completely, feathers and all, with at most a small scatter at the point of capture, was carried off whole by something strong enough to lift and fly or run with an adult hen. That is a short list. An eagle, which can carry a few pounds into a tree or onto a stump to eat. A great horned owl, which can carry a smaller bird but usually eats larger ones on the ground. A coyote, which will carry a hen in its jaws back to cover or to a den. A fox, which does the same, often over a longer distance and sometimes with a tell-tale straight-line trail of a few dropped feathers. A bobcat. What did not carry off the whole bird is the mink, the ermine, the dog, or the skunk. The mink and ermine are too small to remove an adult carcass and will kill in place. The dog kills for chase, not for carrying. The skunk does not take adult birds. So a clean disappearance, feathers and all, points you away from the mustelids and the dog and toward the canids and the large raptors.

A bird that is still there, present and partly or wholly eaten, tells you something completely different. Now you read the body. Where on the body the eating began, and how the bird was killed, are the signatures.

Look at the neck first. A clean set of puncture wounds on the back of the skull and the top of the neck, killing the bird instantly with little wider damage, is the mustelid signature. The mink bites through the skull or the upper neck. So does the ermine. So, often, does the great horned owl, whose talons puncture the skull and the spine from above as it lands on the roosting bird. If the bird was killed cleanly by skull or upper-neck bite and then not eaten, or eaten only at the head and neck, you are almost certainly looking at a mustelid or an owl.

A bird killed by a neck bite that has then been dragged into cover and eaten there, with the carcass partly buried or covered over with leaves and debris, is classic fox. Foxes and coyotes both cache uneaten food — they tuck it under leaves, snow, or loose soil to come back for — and a partly buried chicken carcass dragged toward the fence line or into brush is a strong canid sign. Coyotes tend to eat more at the scene and carry what they can; foxes cache more readily. Both leave the broad, messy, tearing feeding sign of an animal eating meat off a carcass, very different from the precise head-and-neck work of the mustelid.

A bird that has been mauled — wounds across the whole body, broken wings, torn skin, feathers pulled out across a wide area, sometimes the bird still alive but injured and unable to stand — is almost always a dog. Dogs do not kill efficiently. They chase, they grab, they shake, they drop, they chase the next one. The scene of a dog attack is wide and brutal and leaves wounded survivors, which is rare for a wild predator. A wild predator that gets in is there to eat or to kill in the efficient way its body is built for. A dog is there to play out a chase instinct, and the damage shows it.

A bird eaten from the breast, the meat carefully cleaned off the keel bone with the rest of the carcass largely intact, eaten in place, often with the wings and legs left — that is a raptor. Great horned owls and eagles both feed this way, plucking the feathers first to get at the breast meat, leaving the larger bones. The plucking is the tell. Mammalian predators pull feathers out in clumps as they tear; raptors pluck them individually and neatly, and you will find clean, quill-ended feathers in a small pile near the carcass. A raptor kill is a tidy feed.

A bird killed and then, strangely, left largely uneaten — killed and abandoned — is one of two things. Either a predator was interrupted and fled before feeding, which happens with dogs and with canids spooked by a light or a person, or you are looking at surplus killing, which is the mustelid pattern we will spend a whole chapter on. If multiple birds are dead in the coop, killed cleanly by neck or skull bite, and none or almost none have been eaten, you are looking at surplus killing, and the culprit is a mink or an ermine, full stop. Nothing else on your list does that.

Now look at where the body is. A bird killed up on the roost and left there, or pulled off the roost to the floor directly below, points to an owl that dropped onto the roosting bird in the night. A bird killed in the open run, away from the coop, points to a ground predator that came in while the bird was standing about — fox, coyote, dog, or a very bold mink. A bird killed inside a closed coop, with no obvious hole in the wire and the door still latched, points to the squeeze-through predators — the mink and the ermine — because they are the ones that do not need you to have left a door open. A bird that has been dragged partway under the fence, stuck at the wire with its legs pulled through, is a canid trying to extract a meal through the mesh. Each position is a clue about how the predator entered, and how it entered is the clue that tells you what to fix.

Finally, look at the ground around the scene. This is where the photographs pay off, and where snow and mud are your friends. Tracks tell you the rest. A canine track is symmetrical, with four toes and a roughly oval paw print, nail marks in front of the toes, and no opposable thumb — and it is worth knowing that dog tracks and coyote tracks are genuinely hard to tell apart, both being canines, but a coyote's track is more compact and travels in a straighter, more purposeful line, while a domestic dog wanders. A fox track is smaller and neater than either, with a distinct chevron-shaped pad. A mustelid track in snow or mud is unmistakable — it is a bounding, two-by-two pattern, the paired prints of a long animal that moves in hops rather than walks, and it shows up as a line of double dots rather than single footprints. A weasel's tracks look like a stitched line. A mink's, especially near water, look the same but bigger. A skunk's track is plantigrade, long and five-toed, almost like a small bare human foot. A bear track is enormous and obvious and not something you will mistake. A bobcat track is round, with four toes and no nail marks — cats retract their claws — and that absence of nail marks is how you tell a cat track from a canine one at a glance. Raptors leave no tracks at all, but they leave whitewash — their droppings — as splashes on the ground or on the coop roof near where they perched to eat, and a splash of white droppings near plucked feathers is as good as a signature.

Droppings of any kind are worth photographing. Canid scat near the coop, often full of fur and bone, tells you a coyote or fox is working the area. Mink and weasel scat are tiny, dark, twisted, full of bone and fur, often deposited on raised stones along their travel routes in a way that looks deliberate — because it is, they are marking territory. Skunk droppings are larger, crumbly, full of insect parts. None of these will tell you the species with absolute certainty on their own, but combined with the body and the hour, they close the last gap in the identification.

So the method, in one breath. When did it happen — that halves the list. Is the bird there or gone — that halves it again. If it is there, where is the damage and how was it killed — that narrows you to one or two. And what is on the ground, the tracks and the droppings and the position of things, that confirms it. Run those four questions at every scene, and you will know what you are dealing with before you reach for the wire or the trap.

One caution before we move on. You will sometimes find a scene that does not read cleanly. A bird gone with no feathers at all, which means the predator caught it clean and carried it without a struggle — almost certainly a raptor off an open field. A bird dead inside a shut coop with no obvious entry, which means you are looking for a hole the width of your thumb. A scene with two suspects' signs, because a coyote killed and a mink came after, or a dog mauled and an owl cleaned up. Real scenes are messy. The method still works — it just tells you which evidence to weight, and which to treat as secondary. When in doubt, weight the body and the entry point above the scatter. The body almost always tells the truth.

In the next three chapters we go through the cast one group at a time, so the abstract method becomes concrete. We start with the mustelids, because they are the predators most likely to hurt you the most, and because the scene they leave is the one that confuses and sickens new flock owners more than any other. If you remember the first chapter's promise — the three birds killed and none eaten — we are about to explain it.
Chapter 4 - The Small Nocturnal Killers

Here is the scene I promised to explain. You come out in the morning and the coop is intact. The door is shut. The wire is not torn. There is no hole. And inside, on the floor and on the droppings board under the roost, six of your hens are dead. They are not eaten. They are not even moved. They lie where they were, each one killed by a single bite to the back of the head, a tiny dark puncture you almost miss. A couple of them are warm still. The live hens are huddled at the far end, silent and pressed against the wall. There is no blood to speak of. There is no mess. It looks, for all the world, like six birds simply dropped dead in the night from something that touched them once each.

That is the mink scene. Or, if the birds are small — bantams, growing juveniles, chicks — it is the ermine scene. Either way, it is a mustelid scene, and it is the one that breaks new flock owners, because it looks purposeless and cruel in a way that other predation does not. Six birds dead, none eaten. Why would anything do that.

The answer is not cruelty. The answer is a very old instinct meeting an unnatural situation, and once you understand the mechanism, the scene stops being a mystery and starts being a specific, identifiable, preventable event. The instinct is called surplus killing, and it is not unique to mustelids — lions do it in a sealed corral, foxes do it in a seabird colony, bears do it at a salmon run — but it is the mustelids, and especially the mink and the weasel, that bring it into the chicken coop, because they are the predators small enough to get inside one.

Here is how surplus killing works in the brain of a small predator. A mink or a weasel is an animal that hunts constantly. It has a fast metabolism, a small body, and a life organised around finding the next meal, often several times a day. Its hunting instinct is triggered by specific cues — movement, the shape of prey, the sound, the smell — and the animal attacks when those cues fire. In the wild, in the environment the instinct evolved for, each attack leads to a chase, a kill, and then the predator stops to eat or cache what it has caught before hunting again. The cues that trigger an attack and the act of eating are loosely coupled. The cue fires, the animal attacks, and only after the attack does the question of eating come up.

Now put that animal inside a coop with eight roosting hens. The first hen is attacked because it moved, or breathed loudly, or was simply there in front of the mink. The mink kills it with the instinctive killing bite to the back of the skull, the bite that dispatches the prey as fast and as safely as possible. In the wild, the mink would now drag the prey away and eat. But inside the coop, the dead hen is still there, and so are seven live hens, and the live hens are moving — panicked now, shuffling on the roost, fluttering — and each movement fires the attack cue again. The mink kills the second hen. The movement does not stop. It kills the third. The attack cues keep coming because the birds keep moving, and the mink cannot leave, and the instinct does not have a clean off-switch for "you have killed enough." The mink kills until the movement stops, or until the mink is exhausted, or until the cues finally quiet. Then, and only then, does the feeding question arise, and often by then the mink is not even hungry anymore, or it eats from one bird and leaves the rest.

That is the mechanism. It is not malice. It is not bloodlust in any human sense. It is a hunting machine running in an enclosed space with the trigger pulled repeatedly by the presence of more prey than it can process. The birds die because the coop turned a wild hunting sequence into something the instinct was never built for. And the hard truth is that once a mink is inside the coop, this outcome is close to inevitable. You cannot train the mink. You cannot scare it into moderation. The only reliable fix is that the mink never gets inside in the first place.

Which brings us to how they get in, and why this is so demoralising — because the gaps a mink or a weasel can use are gaps you would dismiss. A mink can pass through a round hole about an inch and a quarter across, roughly the diameter of a golf ball. The ermine can go through a gap the width of a United States quarter, less than an inch. Any gap that size — a gap under a poorly fitted door, a knot hole in a board, the seam where two pieces of plywood meet badly, the corner where the wire meets the post but does not quite reach, the opening where a vent was cut and never screened, the drain hole, the gap under the pop door's track — any of these is a door. I have seen coops that a flock owner would swear were tight, that had a one-inch gap at the bottom corner where the frame had warped, and a mink went through it six nights in a row until the flock was gone.

The mink's other great asset is that it climbs. It does not need to dig in the way a fox digs. It climbs wire mesh like a ladder, goes over the top of a four-foot run as a matter of course, and drops in. If the run has no roof, the mink is over the top and inside. If there is a roof but it is attached with gaps at the edges, the mink finds the gap. The ermine is even more committed a climber, and being smaller, exploits even smaller seams. The mental model to hold is that these animals are essentially fluid. They are long, low-slung, flexible bodies that conform to the shape of any opening their skull can pass through, and their skull is small.

Their sign, once you know to look, is distinctive. The mink kills by a bite to the back of the skull or the upper neck, and you will find a small, neat puncture wound set, often two fang marks close together, sometimes hidden under feathers at the nape. There is rarely a struggle, because the bite kills quickly. The birds die where they roost, or are pulled to the floor and left. Multiple birds dead, neat skull bites, no feeding or feeding only at the head — that is mink. An ermine scene is the same but the birds are smaller and the puncture wounds are finer, and sometimes an ermine will drink blood from the neck without eating flesh at all, which is the origin of the old farm phrase about weasels sucking blood. They are not literally sucking, but the wound pattern and the absence of eaten flesh makes it look that way.

Their tracks, in mud or snow, are a giveaway. A mink or weasel moves in bounds, landing on its front feet and bringing its back feet up to where the front feet landed, so the track pattern is a series of paired prints, two-and-two, in a line that looks almost stitched into the surface. In snow you can follow this little dotted line straight back to wherever the animal entered and exited, and you will find the entry point — that one-inch gap, that unscreened vent. In dry dust the pattern is subtler but the two-by-two bounding shows if the light is right. Near water, mink tracks are common along the bank, and if your coop is within a couple of hundred metres of a stream, pond, lake, or the coast — which on Cape Breton means most coops — you are in mink country, full stop.

Why is Cape Breton such good mink habitat? Because mink are semi-aquatic, and the island is wet. Every river valley, every lake edge, every boggy patch, every stretch of coastline, is mink territory. Mink hunt along the water for frogs, fish, muskrats, and nesting waterbirds, and they den in bank holes and old root systems and rock piles. A mink's home range can be a kilometre or more of waterway, and it works that range every night. A coop that sits between a mink's den and its hunting water is a feature on its route, and once the mink has checked it once and found birds, it will check it again. The same is true, at a smaller scale, for the ermine, whose territory is the stone walls and hedgerows and outbuildings where mice live — which is to say, everywhere there are farms and buildings.

The seasonal pattern matters. Mink are more active in some months than others. Young mink disperse from their mothers in late summer and autumn, and these juveniles are naive, hungry, and exploring — and a juvenile mink that blunders into your coop in September is a classic cause of a first surplus-kill event. Ermine are active year-round, and the white winter ermine is if anything harder to see against the snow, and harder still to keep out, because the gaps it uses are so small. Both species are relentless. A mink that has found your coop will be back tomorrow night. There is no version of events where it visits once and decides your birds are not worth it.

So the surplus-kill scene, the one that broke your heart or will, has a complete explanation now. A small, hungry, fast-moving predator with a trigger-happy hunting instinct found a gap into an enclosed space full of moving prey, and its instinct ran the only way it can run in that situation — until the movement stopped. The predator is not evil. The birds were not sacrificed to cruelty. The coop simply failed to be tight enough to exclude an animal the width of your thumb, and the animal did what animals do. The chapter after next — the one on the coop as fortress — is largely about making sure that gap does not exist, and we will spend real time on the half-inch hardware cloth and the skirt and the roof that together make a mink-proof envelope. For now, hold two things. Hold that the mink and the ermine are the predators most likely to produce a catastrophic multi-bird loss on this island, and that they do it not from malice but from a hunting sequence running in an enclosed space. And hold that the gap they used was almost certainly something you would call nothing — a quarter-inch warp, an unscreened vent, a door that did not quite meet the sill. The fix is to find that gap before the mink does.

Before we leave the mustelids, a word about the fisher, which is their larger cousin and which is also present on Cape Breton. The fisher is a bigger mustelid, dark and long, a forest animal that eats porcupines and squirrels and is not commonly a chicken predator — but a fisher that learns your coop exists can and will take birds, and it is strong enough to tear light wire and to pull birds through one-inch mesh. If you are losing birds to something that bites like a mustelid but tears wire like a larger animal, and you live near mature forest, consider the fisher. It is rarer than the mink as a coop predator, but it is on the island, and it is worth keeping in the back of your mind.

We will come back to the mustelids in the trapping chapters, because the mink in particular is one of the few predators for which trapping after the fact is often the only realistic response — once a mink is working your coop, exclusion alone sometimes is not enough, because the mink is already in the habit of visiting, and you will trap to break that habit. But that is the reactive tool. The proactive tool is the coop itself, and we are building toward it. First, the rest of the cast — the ground mammals that take adult birds one at a time rather than all at once.
Chapter 5 - The Diggers And The Crushers

The mustelids kill many birds in one night, and that is the scene people remember. But the more common loss, the one that happens week in and week out across the island, is the single bird taken by a larger ground predator — a coyote, a fox, or far more often than anyone admits, a dog. These animals take one bird at a time, usually, and they take it away, and the scene they leave is less dramatic than the mink scene but no less final for the bird. We are going to spend this chapter on them, because reading their scenes correctly — telling a coyote from a dog from a fox — changes what you do next, and the single most common misdiagnosis in chicken-keeping is blaming a wild predator for what the neighbour's dog actually did.

Start with the Eastern coyote, because it is the one people are most afraid of and the one they most often blame. The Eastern coyote is a real presence on Cape Breton, established across the highlands and the lowlands both, and it is a capable animal — the size of a small German shepherd, thirty to forty-five pounds, with a thick coat, a bushy black-tipped tail, and a track that is a large, oval, four-toed canine print with the nail marks showing in front of the toes. A coyote after chickens will usually work the edges — the dawn and dusk hours, the transition between cover and open ground — and it will approach the coop or the free-ranging flock from downwind, the way a wild canid hunts. When it takes a bird, it kills it with a bite to the neck or the body, carries it off whole in its jaws, and retreats to cover to eat, often caching what it does not finish under leaves or snow.

The scene a coyote leaves is distinctive in a quiet way. There is usually a single dead or missing bird, a scatter of feathers at the point of capture if the bird struggled, a trail of a few more feathers along the line the coyote carried it, and dig marks under the fence if the coyote came in that way. Coyotes are good diggers, and a hole dug under a fence, roughly coyote-sized, with the displaced soil on the outside, is a classic sign. You may find coyote tracks in the soil of the dig and along the approach. You will usually find no surviving wounded birds — a coyote kills cleanly and removes the bird, and the rest of the flock is spooked but uninjured. And you will often find, if you follow the feather trail a little way into the cover, the cache — the uneaten part of the bird tucked under forest debris, where the coyote intended to come back.

Coyote pressure is seasonal. The hardest windows are early spring, when adults are feeding pups in a den and need every calorie, and late autumn through winter, when natural prey is scarcer and a coyote's range widens. A coyote that kills one of your birds in May is almost certainly feeding a litter somewhere within a kilometre or two, and it will be back. A coyote in February is an animal working a harder landscape and willing to take more risk. Both are being exactly what they are — opportunistic predators filling a need — and the response is the same: close the entry, harden the coop and the run, and treat the bird as the tuition for a lesson about your fence.

The red fox is the coyote's smaller, bolder cousin, and on Cape Breton it is the more common canid chicken predator, because foxes live in and around human settlement in a way coyotes largely do not. A red fox will den near houses, will cross an open yard in daylight, and will work a coop or a free-ranging flock with a patience that borders on contempt. Foxes take a single bird at a time, often over several consecutive nights, and the pattern is recognisable: one bird gone each dusk or dawn for three or four nights running, until you change something. A fox will dig under a fence — its dig is smaller and neater than a coyote's, sometimes just a scraped-out slot under the bottom rail — and it will also climb, in the sense of going over a low or insecure fence by scrambling up the wire or using a nearby object. Fox tracks are smaller and neater than a coyote's, with a distinct V-shaped or chevron central pad, and they travel in a tighter, more direct line. The carried bird leaves a feather trail, and the fox will cache what it does not eat — sometimes surprisingly close to the coop, under a shed or a woodpile, which is a useful clue if you find it.

Foxes have a habit that coyotes do not, and it matters for identification. A fox that learns your coop is a food source will often come back night after night, taking one bird each time, until the food source is gone or excluded. This is the "serial" pattern — a slow bleed of one bird a night — and it is the signature of a fox more than any other predator on your list. If you are losing exactly one bird every night or two, and the bird is gone clean with a feather trail leading off, suspect the fox. A coyote more often takes what it needs and moves on for a few days.

Both coyotes and foxes are outcompeted and outnumbered, as chicken predators, by the domestic dog — and here is where I want you to slow down and read carefully, because this is the misdiagnosis I warned you about. The loose domestic dog is the single most destructive predator of backyard chickens in North America, and Cape Breton is no exception, and almost nobody who loses birds to a dog believes, in the first moment, that it was a dog. They blame the coyote. They blame the fox. They blame the eagle. They do not want to blame a neighbour's pet, and they do not recognise the dog scene, because it does not look the way they expect predation to look.

Here is what a dog does. A loose dog, finding a flock in a yard or a field, does not kill in the efficient, food-getting way a wild predator kills. A dog kills in play, and in chase, and in the excitement of movement. A dog will chase the birds, snap at them, grab them, shake them, drop them, and chase the next one. It will run the flock across a wide area, exhausting and mauling birds as it goes, and it will kill several in an afternoon without eating any of them. A dog attack leaves a distinctive scene: birds dead or wounded scattered across a wide area, often far from the coop; birds with broad, messy injuries — broken wings, torn skin, feather loss across the whole body rather than at a single bite point; and wounded survivors, birds that are injured but alive, which is rare for a wild predator that got inside. The dog is not hungry in the way that triggers feeding. The dog is running an instinct that has no off-switch in the presence of fleeing animals.

The dog scene is wide, brutal, and wasteful in a way wild predation is not, and recognising it matters because the response is entirely different. You do not trap a neighbour's dog. You do not shoot a neighbour's dog as a first response. You talk to the neighbour, you check the local bylaws on loose dogs and livestock, you improve your fence, and you consider whether your birds are safe in that yard at all. The legal and social situation around dogs is a thing of its own, and it is not the same as the situation around a wild coyote. A surprising number of "coyote problems" on this island are dog problems in disguise, and the people who figure that out are the people who stop losing birds.

A word about dog tracks. A domestic dog's track is, as I said, genuinely hard to tell from a coyote's — both are symmetrical, four-toed canine prints with nail marks — but a dog's track tends to be a little splayed, a little rounder, and the trail wanders where a coyote's travels purposefully. A dog walks where its nose takes it. A coyote walks somewhere. If the trail of tracks in your yard looks like a dog's afternoon — looping, investigating, going to the door, coming back — it was a dog. If the trail enters at one point, goes straight to the coop, and leaves by the same route, it may have been a coyote, or it may have been a very focused dog. Use the body and the scatter to confirm. A wide, scattered, wounded-survivor scene is a dog, regardless of the tracks.

The black bear is a different order of problem, and it is mercifully rarer. Bears on Cape Breton are present in good numbers in the forested interior and the highlands, and a bear that finds a coop will not politely dig under the wire or squeeze through a gap. A bear will tear the structure apart. Chicken wire, light welded wire, thin plywood, plastic connectors — none of it slows a bear that has decided your coop has food. A bear attack scene is unmistakable: the coop or run is damaged structurally, panels torn off, posts bent, wire ripped, feeders pulled out and emptied, and birds dead or scattered. You will know. The fix for a bear is not a better latch. The fix for a bear is electric fencing, a secure feed-storage regime that does not spread smell, and in serious cases a conversation with Natural Resources about a problem animal. We will spend real time on the electric fence in its own chapter, because no other tool in this book works against as wide a range of the cast, from a bear down to a raccoon-class animal.

Bears are most likely to hit you in late summer and fall, during the hyperphagia period when they are eating everything they can to put on fat for denning. A bear in September is an eating machine operating with single-minded intensity, and a coop with accessible feed or a run with visible birds is a target. By contrast, a bear in spring is less likely to bother — it has just come out of the den lean and is grazing on greens and early berries — and a bear in midwinter is asleep. Time your vigilance to the bear's calendar.

The bobcat is the last of the ground mammals worth naming here, and it is an occasional rather than a common chicken predator on Cape Breton. Bobcats are present in the rougher country and the highland margins, and they are capable predators — a bobcat will take a free-ranging bird, especially in winter when other prey is scarce. The bobcat scene is similar to a coyote's in some ways — a single bird taken, killed with a bite, possibly cached — but the tracks give it away. A bobcat track is round, with four toes and no nail marks, because cats retract their claws when they walk. If you have round tracks with no nails, you have a cat, and the cat on this island big enough to take a chicken is the bobcat. Bobcat pressure is low for most backyard flocks, but it exists, and it is worth distinguishing from a coyote so you do not spend a week defending against the wrong animal.

Notice what ties these animals together. They all take adult birds, usually one at a time. They all approach on the ground, by day, by dusk, or by dawn. They all leave a body or a feather trail rather than a silent coop full of the dead. And they are all, with the exception of the bear and the dog, behaving as opportunistic wild predators doing what their bodies and their hunger tell them to do. The coyote is feeding a den in May. The fox has learned a slow food source. The bobcat is working a hard winter. The dog is the outlier — not hungry, not wild, but running a chase instinct that has no brake — and the bear is the other outlier, a structural damage event rather than a careful kill. Read the scene, separate them, and you will know whether to fix the fence, talk to the neighbour, or string the electric wire.

The ground mammals are the everyday face of chicken loss on this island. The mustelids are the catastrophic one-off. The next chapter is the third face — the predators that come from above, the raptors, because Cape Breton's bald eagle density makes the sky a real part of your threat picture in a way it simply is not in most of the continent.
Chapter 6 - From Above

The predators in this chapter do not dig, and they do not squeeze through gaps. They drop out of the sky, and they change the shape of your defence, because everything you build against a ground predator — the tight fence, the buried skirt, the latched door — is irrelevant to an animal that simply flies over it. On Cape Breton the raptor threat is real and specific, and it is led by a bird most people do not think of as a chicken predator at all: the bald eagle.

The bald eagle is common on this island. Not present — common. Cape Breton has one of the densest bald eagle populations in North America, concentrated along the coast and around lakes and rivers, and the birds are year-round residents, not seasonal migrants. They are large — a female bald eagle has a wingspan over two metres, six and a half feet, and weighs nine to twelve pounds — and they are powerful enough to take an adult standard-size hen off an open field. Most people think of bald eagles as fish eaters and scavengers, and they are both, but they are also opportunistic hunters of live prey, and a free-ranging chicken in an open yard is well within their capability. The eagle does not need your bird. It simply needs to notice it, and on Cape Breton the eagles notice a great deal.

The bald eagle kill is a daylight event. It happens most often in the morning, when the bird is actively hunting, and it happens over open ground — a mown lawn, a pasture, a field edge — where the eagle has a clear approach and a clear drop. An eagle takes a hen by stooping — coming in at an angle, feet forward — and striking with the talons. A large hen is heavy for an eagle to carry far, and the eagle often kills or immobilises the bird on the ground and then eats it there, or carries it in stages to a feeding perch. What you find at the scene is the strike point — a shallow crater in the grass where the talons and the body hit, surrounded by a burst of feathers — and sometimes the carcass nearby, opened at the breast, the meat cleanly eaten, the large bones and the wings left. If the eagle has carried the bird off, you find only the strike point and the feather burst, and the bird is gone.

The signature of a raptor kill, as we said in the identification chapter, is plucking. Raptors pluck feathers individually and neatly before feeding, and the feathers at a raptor kill are clean, whole quills in a small concentrated pile, not the clumped and torn feathers of a mammalian feed. You will also often find whitewash — the white, chalky droppings of a large bird — splashed on the ground, on a fence post, or on the coop roof near where the bird perched to eat. Whitewash and plucked feathers together are as close to a signature as predation leaves. There are no tracks, because the predator did not walk in. There is no dig. There is no gap in the wire, because the bird was taken from above the fence. The absence of mammalian sign, combined with the strike point and the plucking, tells you it came from the air.

The great horned owl is the nighttime counterpart, and it is your main nocturnal raptor threat. Great horned owls are large, powerful birds — even heavier than a bald eagle, three to four pounds of dense hunting muscle — and they are ambush predators that hunt from a perch at the edge of woods or a large tree, dropping silently onto prey up to the size of a rooster or a rabbit. A great horned owl can take a roosting bird from an unsecured run on a half-lit night, killing it instantly with talons driven into the skull and the spine from above, and you will find the bird on the ground under the roost with deep puncture wounds in the back and the skull, plucked at the breast if the owl had time to feed. Great horned owls hunt the first hours after dusk and the last hours before dawn, and they are silent — there is usually no call, no warning, just the impact and the absence.

The great horned owl scene can be confused with the mustelid scene, because both kill by skull or upper-neck damage and both can leave a bird under the roost. The distinction is the wound pattern. The owl kills with talons — large, paired, deeply penetrating punctures from above, often four talon marks in the pattern of the foot. The mink kills with teeth — a bite, leaving two smaller, closer puncture marks from the canines. The owl may pluck and feed on the breast. The mink rarely feeds at all. If the bird is on the ground under the roost with deep puncture wounds and a plucked breast, think owl first. If there are several birds dead, each with a small bite, and none eaten, think mink.

Then there are the hawks, and on Cape Breton the hawk threat is real but narrower. The Cooper's hawk and the sharp-shinned hawk are the two you will see around the flock, and they are bird-hunting specialists — accipiters, built for speed and agility through trees, with short rounded wings and a long tail for steering. They are the hawks that chase songbirds through the yard, and they are the hawks that will hit a free-ranging flock at midday, going for chicks, bantams, and small juveniles. A Cooper's hawk can take a full-grown bantam outright and can injure a standard-size bird, but for adult standard birds the accipiters are less of a primary threat than the eagle. The red-tailed hawk is also here — a large, broad-winged buteo, the roadside hawk on a telephone pole — and it can take a small bird but is more likely to be hunting rodents in your field than your chickens in your run. Treat the hawks as a real threat to anything chick-sized or bantam-sized that is ranging free, and a lower threat to adult standard birds.

There is a legal reality about all of these birds that you must understand before you think about any response, and it is this. Every raptor on this list — the eagle, the owl, every hawk — is protected by federal law in Canada under the Migratory Birds Convention Act and, for some species, additional provincial protection. In the United States the equivalent is the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act and the Migratory Bird Treaty Act. You cannot trap, relocate, injure, or kill a raptor because it took a chicken. There is no "it was eating my livestock" exception that lets you shoot an eagle. The legal and ethical response to raptor predation is exclusion and deterrence, full stop. You cover the run. You provide overhead cover for the free-rangers. You use netting or solid roofing or shade cloth to deny the diving bird a clean strike. You do not, under any circumstance, harm the bird. We will come back to this in the prevention and trapping chapters, because the raptor constraint shapes what tools are even available to you, and it is a constraint you need to carry forward honestly.

That legal constraint is also a reason to take prevention seriously. You can trap a mink that is hitting your coop. You cannot trap the eagle that is hitting your field. The only thing you can do about the eagle is deny it the opportunity — overhead netting, a covered run, trees and shrubs that break up its dive lane, a guardian animal that keeps the flock alert and moving. The raptor chapters of your defence have to be built, not trapped, because the law gives you no trapping option and because, frankly, the eagles and the owls were here first and are doing what eagles and owls do.

A practical note on identification before we move on. Bald eagles and great horned owls are big enough that you will often see them — an eagle perched in a dead tree near the field at midmorning, an owl silhouetted on a fence post at the wood's edge at last light, the great wide shape lifting off as you walk out. The hawks are smaller and faster, and you will more often see the aftermath than the bird. But all of them leave the sign we have described — the strike point, the plucked feathers, the whitewash, the puncture wounds — and that sign is how you know, even when you never see the bird itself.

The sky is a real part of the Cape Breton threat picture. In many places on the continent, raptors are a minor concern compared to mammals. Here, with the bald eagle density we have, the daytime sky deserves a place in your mental model alongside the coyote and the fox and the mink. A free-ranging flock on an open Cape Breton field, with no overhead cover and no guardian, is exposed to one of the most capable daytime predators on the continent, and the losses people attribute to "something" — the vague, unseen daytime disappearance — are very often eagle losses that were never correctly read.

We have one more short chapter on the smaller opportunists, the rats and the crows, and then we will have finished the cast and we can turn to the main work of the book, which is the prevention. By the end of the next chapter you will know every animal that can cost you birds on this island, day or night, large or small, and you will be ready to build against them.
Chapter 7 - The Opportunists

The cast has one more tier, and it is the tier that will rarely kill an adult hen but will quietly cost you the next generation. Rats, and to a lesser extent crows, are the opportunists — the animals that take the eggs from under a setting hen, the chicks from a brooder, the feed from the feeder, and the occasional small juvenile that strays too far from the flock. They are not the predators that end a flock in a night. They are the predators that hollow out a breeding season and attract the larger predators you actually fear. They deserve their own short chapter, because the fix for them is a different set of habits, and because ignoring them is how a small problem becomes a mink problem.

The Norway rat is the rat you are most likely to have, and it is present on Cape Breton wherever there are people, buildings, and feed. Rats are not primarily chicken predators — they are omnivores that eat grain, compost, garden produce, pet food, garbage, and, when they can get it, animal protein. A rat will tunnel under a coop floor to reach spilled feed, and once under the floor it will take eggs from under a setting hen, kill chicks in a brooder, and gnaw at juvenile birds roosting low. An adult standard hen is far too large for a rat to kill, and a healthy flock will itself hunt and kill rats that get bold — chickens are not passive about a small mammal in the run — but eggs and chicks are vulnerable, and a serious rat population will eat into your hatch rate and your replacement birds in a way that looks, at first, like bad luck.

The rat sign is distinctive. Rat burrows are round holes about two inches across, often at the base of a wall, under a coop, or at the edge of a compost pile, with a worn, smooth entrance from repeated use. Rat droppings are dark, capsule-shaped, about half an inch long, found in latrine areas near the burrows and along the rat's travel routes. You will often find gnawed wood — rats gnaw constantly to wear their teeth — at the edges of doors, feed bins, and floor seams. In the brooder, the sign of a rat predation event is a chick gone missing overnight with no other explanation, sometimes with a small blood spot or a few down feathers at the edge of the brooder, and sometimes a bitten, injured chick that did not die immediately. A rat takes what it can carry.

What makes rats a chicken-predator problem, indirectly, is what they attract. Rats are food. A coop with a rat population is advertising, in the smell-and-sound language of the night, that there is easy prey here. Foxes eat rats. Coyotes eat rats. Mink eat rats. A rat problem around your coop is a slow magnet for the very predators you are trying to keep away, and clearing the rats is not just about the eggs and the chicks — it is about removing the first link in a chain that ends with a mink in your coop. This is worth saying plainly, because people are often puzzled that a mink "suddenly" found their coop, when the mink was following the rats that had been there for months.

The fix for rats is mostly a discipline of feed and habitat. Feed is the draw. Spilled feed on the ground, open feed bags in the coop, feeders left full overnight, compost piles with food scraps near the run — all of these are rat magnets. The discipline is to feed what the birds will clean up in a day, to store feed in sealed metal or heavy plastic bins that rats cannot chew through, to remove or secure the feeder overnight, and to keep compost well away from the coop. The habitat fix is to remove the shelter rats need — tall grass and weeds around the coop perimeter, stacked lumber and junk piles near the run, gaps under sheds where rats can burrow. A clear, mown perimeter around the coop is unattractive to a rat, because rats fear open ground. If you have a persistent rat population after the feed and habitat discipline, then rat-specific traps — snap traps in protected boxes, placed where only rats will reach them — are the targeted tool, and they work well. Poison is a poor choice around a flock, because of the risk to the chickens, to pets, and to the predators that eat the dying rats, and because a poisoned rat dying under your coop is a smell you do not want. Snap traps, placed and checked, are the humane and effective option.

Crows are the other opportunist worth a mention, and they are a minor concern for adult birds but a real one for eggs and chicks. The American crow is common on Cape Breton, intelligent and social, and a crow will take an egg from an unsecured nest box if it can reach it, and it will take a chick from a free-ranging flock if the chick is small and the crow is bold. Crows are protected under the Migratory Birds Convention Act, like the raptors, and the response to them is exclusion rather than trapping. A crow that learns your coop has accessible eggs is a crow that will return with friends, and a small flock of crows working a run can clean out a nest box line in a morning. The fix is to keep nest boxes where crows cannot reach them — inside the coop, behind the pop hole, not in an open-fronted shelter — and to provide cover for the hen and chicks during the brood-rearing weeks.

There is one more name worth a brief mention in this chapter, and that is the domestic cat. I have not put the cat on the main cast list, because the domestic and feral cat is a very minor predator of adult chickens on Cape Breton and is more of a predator of chicks and juveniles. A pet cat will occasionally take a chick. A feral cat is a more serious concern for a broody hen with chicks on open range. But the cat is not a primary predator of an established flock in the way the mustelids and canids are, and I mention it here only so you know I have not forgotten it. If you are losing chicks to a cat, the fix is the same as for any small predator — cover the brooder, protect the broody hen and her chicks, and address the cat as a neighbour or, in the feral case, as a separate issue.

That is the whole cast. Eagles, owls, and hawks from above. Coyotes, foxes, dogs, bears, and the occasional bobcat on the ground. Mink and ermine in the small hours. Skunks for the eggs. Rats for the eggs and the chicks. Crows as opportunists. The cat as a minor chick predator. These are the animals that can cost you birds on this island, and nothing else of consequence.

You now have the inventory and the identification method. You can walk a scene and read it. You know who is actually out there, and you know who is not. The next part of the book is the long, practical heart of the matter — how you build and maintain and live with a flock in this landscape so that the cast we have just met stays outside the wire, and so that when one of them does get through, you know what to do about it. We start with the coop itself, because the coop is your first and best defence, and the materials you choose for it will do more to determine your losses than any trap you ever set.
Chapter 8 - The Coop Is Your First And Best Defence

Everything in the first seven chapters was diagnosis. From here on, the book is about what to do, and we start with the coop, because the coop is what prevents the most losses for the most flocks for the longest time. The coop and the attached run, built right, will keep out almost everything on the Cape Breton cast. Built wrong, it is a feeder. The difference between right and wrong comes down to a few specific materials and a few specific design choices, and the single most important of them — the one that separates a coop that holds from a coop that fails — is the wire you choose.

There is a material called hardware cloth, and if you take one practical thing from this book, take this. Hardware cloth is welded wire mesh made from heavy galvanised steel wire, woven into a tight grid and welded at every intersection, and it is sold in rolls at any farm and building supply store in half-inch and quarter-inch grid sizes. Half-inch means the squares of the grid are half an inch across. Quarter-inch means they are a quarter of an inch. It is stiff, it is strong, it cannot be torn by a fox or a coyote, it cannot be chewed through by a mink, and — this is the part that matters most — the half-inch grid is too small for a mink to pass through, and the quarter-inch grid is too small for an ermine. Hardware cloth is the material that makes a coop a fortress rather than a suggestion. It is not cheap, and it is the place where flock owners cheapskate, and it is the place where they almost all go wrong.

The material people use instead, and the material almost every coop plan on the internet assumes, is chicken wire. Chicken wire is the thin, flexible, hexagonal-mesh wire that has been associated with chickens for a hundred years. It is cheap, it is light, it is easy to work with, and it is almost useless against any predator that matters. Chicken wire will keep a chicken in. It will not keep a predator out. A fox will chew through chicken wire in minutes. A coyote will tear it. A mink will go through the inch or two-inch mesh of chicken wire as if it were not there, because the gaps are larger than the mink's skull. A raccoon — and I know we do not have them on Cape Breton in numbers, but the principle holds — will reach through chicken wire and pull a roosting bird's head through the mesh piece by piece. Chicken wire is a fence for the chicken, not a wall against the predator, and the number of coops that are wrapped in chicken wire and called predator-proof is the number of coops that will eventually fail.

The grid size is where the Cape Breton reality sharpens the decision. A mink passes through any hole the width of its skull, which is about an inch and a quarter for an adult mink. A one-inch grid lets a mink through. A half-inch grid does not. This is not a marginal call. A half-inch hardware cloth on every opening — every window, every vent, every gap between frame members — excludes the mink, which is the predator most likely to produce the catastrophic surplus-kill event. Quarter-inch excludes the ermine too. If you are building or retrofitting, the half-inch is the standard, and the quarter-inch is the choice for the smallest gaps and the brooder where the very small threats matter most. Do not use one-inch hardware cloth on a Cape Breton coop and call it mink-proof. It is not.

The wire goes everywhere the coop is open to the air. Windows, yes, but also the high vents under the eaves, the gap where the roof overhangs the wall, the opening for the pop hole when the door is open during the day, and the full surface of the attached run. Every one of these is a door to a mink or an ermine, and every one of them needs the half-inch cloth, attached to the frame with screws and washers or poultry staples driven flush, not the loose staples that leave a gap at the edge. A mink will test every seam of your coop, working the edge where the wire meets the wood, looking for the place the staple did not hold and the mesh pulls away. Attach the cloth tight, on the outside of the frame, with fasteners every few inches, and check the seams every season, because wood moves and staples loosen and a gap that was not there in June can be there by October.

The run attached to the coop needs the same material, and it needs a roof. A run without a roof is not a defence against the Cape Breton cast — it is a holding pen. The mink and the ermine climb. The raptors drop in. A four-foot-high run with hardware-cloth walls and no top is mink-accessible from above as a matter of routine, and a six-foot run is no different. The roof can be solid — plywood, polycarbonate sheet, metal roofing — for the weather shelter and the total raptor and mink exclusion, and it can be hardware cloth for the rest of the run, which keeps out everything that climbs or drops while letting light and air through. A common and effective pattern is a solid roof over the coop and a section of the run, with hardware-cloth roofing over the remainder, all fully enclosed. The principle is that there is no open top anywhere in the birds' secure envelope.

What about the lighter overhead material, the plastic bird netting or the deer netting that many people stretch over a run? It has a use, and a limit. Plastic netting stops a diving raptor — an eagle or a hawk stooping onto the run will pull up or bounce off the netting, and for the free-ranging flock a strung net is a real raptor deterrent. But netting does not stop a climbing predator. A mink goes over the wall of the run and through the netting or around its edge without slowing down. Netting is an overhead-raptor defence, not an overhead-mink defence, and if your run has netting instead of hardware-cloth roofing, you have solved the eagle problem and kept the mink problem. On Cape Breton, where both are real, the choice between netting and hardware-cloth roofing comes down to which predator you are willing to leave unaddressed, and the honest answer is usually that the mink is the one you cannot leave unaddressed. Netting belongs over the open range. Hardware cloth belongs over the run.

The next layer is the apron, sometimes called the skirt, and it is the answer to the diggers. Foxes, coyotes, and dogs dig under fences, and they dig at the base of the fence where the wire meets the ground. The naive defence is to bury the wire — to dig a trench a foot deep and run the wire down into it — and that works, but it is hard labour, and it has a failure mode, which is that a determined animal will dig deeper than you dug. The apron is the better answer. An apron is a horizontal skirt of hardware cloth extending outward from the base of the fence, lying flat on the ground or just under a thin layer of soil, typically twelve to eighteen inches wide. An animal approaching the fence to dig hits the apron first, and because the apron is lying flat, the animal cannot dig down at the fence line — it has to start its dig a foot or more back from the fence, which most animals do not do, because their instinct is to dig at the base of the wall they are trying to get under. The apron defeats the dig without the trench.

A simple apron is made by taking a roll of hardware cloth, running it vertically up the wall of the run as the wall cladding for the bottom foot or so, and then bending the bottom edge outward to lie flat on the ground for another foot, forming an L-shape. Secure the flat part to the ground with landscape staples or pegs, and let the grass grow through it. Within a season it disappears into the turf and becomes a permanent, invisible defence. If you are retrofitting, you can lay a skirt of hardware cloth flat against the outside of an existing fence and peg it down. The width matters — twelve inches stops most foxes and dogs, eighteen inches stops most coyotes, and no reasonable apron stops a bear, which will go through the fence rather than under it. For the bear, you need electric, which we will come to.

There is one more design choice that is easy to overlook and that costs people birds, and that is the coop floor. A wooden floor raised on skids or blocks, with hardware cloth over every vent, is secure against everything that does not chew through wood — which is, for practical purposes, everything on your list except a bear and a serious rat population. A dirt floor, or a floor of loose pallets over dirt, is a mink and rat and mouse highway, because these animals simply tunnel up through the soil under the coop and through the gaps. A solid wooden floor, or a concrete slab, denies the underground approach. If you must have a dirt floor, line it with half-inch hardware cloth laid flat under a few inches of bedding, with the edges sealed to the wall — a kind of apron under the whole coop. The principle is the same as the wall apron: deny the dig.

So the fortress coop, in one breath, is a solid-floored structure with every opening — window, vent, pop hole, eaves gap — covered in half-inch hardware cloth fastened tight to the frame, an attached run built of the same material with a hardware-cloth or solid roof, and a horizontal apron of hardware cloth extending a foot or more outward at the base of every wall. Built this way, the coop is a sealed envelope that excludes every predator on the Cape Breton cast except the bear and the neighbour's dog that comes through the gate you left open. The bear is the electric-fence problem. The open gate is the habits problem. We will come to both. But the coop, built right, is the layer that does the daily work, and it is the layer most often built wrong, and it is the layer worth spending the money and the time on before anything else.

I want to say a word about the cost, because the cost is the reason people cheapskade on the hardware cloth, and the cost is worth confronting directly. Half-inch hardware cloth is roughly two to three times the price of chicken wire per square foot, and a fully enclosed run can take a significant amount of it. A coop that uses hardware cloth everywhere will cost meaningfully more to build than one wrapped in chicken wire. This is true. It is also true that one surplus-kill event, one night of a mink inside the coop, costs you a significant fraction of your flock — birds you raised from chicks, birds that took six months to come into lay, birds with names — and the hardware cloth is the difference between that night happening and not happening. The material is not where to save. Save on the paint. Save on the decorative trim. Do not save on the wire.

There is a deeper point here about defence in layers that the whole rest of the book will exercise, and I want to name it now so it is in your head as we go. No single layer is enough on its own. The hardware cloth on the walls is not enough without the roof, because the mink climbs. The roof is not enough without the apron, because the fox digs. The apron is not enough without the door discipline, because the door you left open defeats every other layer at once.
