It is the landscape you chose to keep chickens in, and it is a good landscape, full of life, and the same wildness that makes Cape Breton a beautiful place to live is the wildness that makes it a hard place to keep an undefended flock.

The realistic work, then, is not to rid your property of predators, which is neither possible nor desirable. The realistic work is to make your flock a harder target than the other food sources around you, so that the coyote hunting the river valley finds the mice and the rabbits and the deer easier than your birds, and the mink working the stream finds the frogs and the muskrats easier than your coop, and the eagle soaring over the field finds the fish and the carrion easier than your hens. Every predator on this island is an opportunist, and opportunists take the easy thing. Make your birds the hard thing, and the predators will mostly take the easy things, and you will lose the occasional bird instead of the regular bird, and the occasional bird is a loss you can live with.

The way you make your birds the hard thing is the defence in layers we have spent the book building, and it is worth recapping the whole stack here at the end, as a single mental model rather than a chapter-by-chapter list. The fortress coop is the centre — a solid-floored structure with half-inch hardware cloth on every opening, a roofed run of the same material, and an apron extending a foot or more out from every wall. The door and latch are positive, a carabiner through the hasp, and an automatic door handles the dusk deadline when you cannot. The dusk discipline — count, shut, check — is the nightly habit that turns the fortress into an actual defence. The feed is pulled at dusk, so the coop does not advertise itself through the night. The free-range question, if you range, is managed with overhead cover to break the raptor's stoop, with a moveable tractor or a netted range to give the birds ground under a roof, and with electric netting or a strand fence to push the ground predators out beyond the coop. The electric fence is grounded, jouled, vegetation-cleared, and tested weekly through the active season, and it is retired for the deep winter when the snow buries its lower strands. The trapping tools — the cage trap, the weasel box, the bodygrip in a controlled set — sit in reserve for the specific, repeat predator that defeats the exclusion, and they are used with the legal and ethical seriousness the law and the welfare demand. That is the whole stack, and the flock that sits inside the full stack is a hard target, and the flock that sits inside a partial stack is the one that gets hit.

The seasonal rhythm is part of the long view, and it is worth naming as a calendar you carry in your head. Spring is the high-pressure season, because the adult predators are feeding young and every calorie matters, and the daylight is lengthening and the weather is breaking and the flock is eager to range. This is when the coyote with a den full of pups is most motivated to test your fence, and when the fox is most persistent in working your run, and when the eagles are most active feeding their own nestlings. Late spring into early summer is the juvenile-dispersal window for the smaller predators, when the young mink and weasels are leaving their mothers and exploring, naive and hungry, and a juvenile mustelid finding your coop for the first time is a classic cause of a surplus-kill event in August or September. Late summer and autumn is the bear's hyperphagia window, when the bears are fattening for denning and the calories are everything, and a coop with accessible feed is a target. Winter is the scarcity window, when the daylight is short, the natural prey is thin, the snow is deep, and the predators are working harder for less — and your electric fence is compromised by the snow, and your birds are slow and cold and spending more time in the run. Each season has its pressures, and the vigilant flock owner matches the defence to the season, tightening the range in the high-pressure weeks and relaxing it in the quiet ones.

There is an emotional side to this that the book has not much addressed, and it is worth a word at the end, because losing birds is hard in a way that losing a tomato crop is not, and the flock owners who stay in it long-term are the ones who have found a way to carry the losses without being broken by them. A hen you raised from a chick, that you have fed and watched and named, that comes when you call and follows you around the yard, is a small relationship, and its death is a small grief, and a coop that is hit hard — the surplus-kill scene, the dog attack, the slow bleed of a fox taking a bird a night — is a real loss. The anger and the guilt we talked about in the first chapter are real, and they come back with each loss, and the long view has to make room for them. The frame that I have found useful, and that I offer you, is to treat each loss as the tuition for the lesson it teaches — the door that was left open teaches the dusk discipline, the gap in the wire teaches the hardware-cloth standard, the mink that got in teaches the half-inch mesh. The bird is gone, and the grief is real, and the lesson is the thing you can take from it that makes the next flock safer. The flock owners who quit are often the ones who could not turn the loss into the lesson, and the flock owners who stay are the ones who did, and the difference is not the size of the loss but the relationship to it.

There is also a point at which the right move is to ask for help, and the book would be incomplete if it did not name it. The Nuisance Wildlife Control Operator, the NWCO, is a licensed professional who handles exactly the situations this book has described — the repeat predator, the bear that has learned the coop, the mink that cannot be excluded, the caught animal you do not want to dispatch. An NWCO is equipped, authorised, and current on the law in a way that a book cannot be, and the cost of hiring one is, for many situations, less than the cost of the continued losses or the legal risk of handling it yourself. Nova Scotia's Department of Natural Resources and Renewables, NRR, is the authority on the law and the status of species, and they are the right call for questions about what is permitted and for the reporting of any situation that involves a protected species, a sick animal, or a human safety concern. The flock owner who knows when to call is the flock owner who solves the hard problems well.

I want to end where we started, with the morning you find feathers, because the whole book has been a way of turning that morning from a moment of shock and guesswork into a moment of method. You walk out with the coffee and the feed bucket, and something is wrong. You secure the survivors, and you photograph the scene, and you notice the hour and the weather and the position of the body, and you read the four questions — when, where, what, and how — and by the time you have finished the first coffee you know, not guess, what you are dealing with. You know which predator, you know how it got in, you know which layer of the defence failed, and you know what to fix. You make the fix. You tighten the layer. You move on, with the loss carried honestly and the lesson applied, and the flock behind you is harder to hit tomorrow than it was yesterday. That is the work. It is good work. It is the work of keeping a small piece of domestic life alive in a landscape that is full of wild life, and doing it in a way that respects both, and the predators are not your enemies in it. They are your neighbours, and the fence is the boundary, and the boundary is what makes the neighbourhood work.

The feathers on the grass are where it started, for you and for everyone who keeps birds here. Take the photographs. Read the scene. Build the layers. Check the door. And the flock you have next year will be the one that survived, not because you eliminated the predators, but because you made the flock the harder choice. That is enough. That is the whole craft.
Go count your birds.
